speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five of an interview with Masha Frydman Zeleznikow. OK. At the end of the last tape, we was talking about how difficult life in Australia was, especially when your son was sick in an infectious disease hospital, Fairfield.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So it was-- it was very sad that was all-- you know, my-- my family couldn't help me much. And anybody really-- nobody could help me. Because my father was a widower. He didn't sort-- know the language. To the end of his days, he never learned decent English. He didn't have money. And we had such a terrible thing, it was a little child in a hospital that I didn't know what was happening. Until one day I got a letter. Even not a telephone call, I got a letter that I can come and visit him. And we didn't have a car, of course. But we still managed to go to Fairfield, the three of us, my son, my husband, and my father. And then for the first time after weeks and weeks, I saw the little boy. And the first thing that he told me in Yiddish was, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. I've got-- you've got a head on your [NON-ENGLISH]. And look, for us, it was really-- we were very happy. We forgot all the problems in the world. And that showed me first that he is reasonable all right, but he wasn't all right altogether. He had one side weaker than the other one. But you couldn't see. As he was growing older, it became more obvious. Still, it was a lot of exercise, and this is what he picked up running. He was a very determined little boy. And we did a lot of exercise. And life went more or less back to normal. It was very hard. Because again, because Abram didn't have that job that we were desperate for him to have. And I had John after polio, when he needed to exercise. But still I did managed to get some work home. I managed to get like some jackets, I was sewing when John was asleep, I was sitting and sewing. And at night I was making some jackets and skirts, and whatever. And that was for the next-- that was for the next year and a half. And I even-- John started kindergarten. And then one day when I picked him up from kindergarten, I suddenly noticed that he is limping. And I didn't know, either it's poliomyelitis, or it's something that I put a rush to a doctor, and it turned out after all the tests that were done that he had something which was called Perthes disease. And he had to go for two years to a hospital. And he was in a splint for two years in Mount Eliza, which was terrible, you know. Then the Children's Hospital for the long-- if they had a case that had to be longer in hospital, they had it in Mount Eliza, they had a part of the Children's Hospital. And you can imagine the situation where John was in Mount Eliza. We didn't have a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so first of all, we're terribly upset. When-- because my English was still very poor at that stage. When I heard-- that they told me a grave disease. I ask, is it a very serious one? And the doctor said to me. It's a grave disease. And I somehow connect the grave. And that-- and this is the tragedy of my [NON-ENGLISH], you know, when they didn't have interpreters at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so John was in Mount Eliza. Abram was working wherever he could, and I started to work. Because John was now permanent in hospital. I started to work for full time. And so that is the better situation. Because, so we were able, the two of us were working. Even if we didn't make quite a lot of money, but still two pensions was more than we had before, which was nothing. And John started-- so like-- so John was in Mount Eliza almost two years. Because John started school. He was still on crutches. He was-- when he started school, which was seven, he was seven years, he was still on crutches. So it was quite a few years that he was in the hospital and on crutches. And in meantime, I so-- when John came out of hospital, I started again to take work at home at this stage. We got-- we still-- we didn't have a house. We couldn't afford it. But we had a little-- we rented a little house. And we were living very poor. This was in Brunswick. And I started to learn English. Because I realized that without language, I'm not going to go far. So I learned English as much as I could. I went to courses or whatever I could. And I became pregnant with Janet. And still-- so my mind was constantly working on it. I was thinking that we will not be able to survive. Because if I have another child, then with one, it was whatever Abram is earning is not good enough. And we started to look for a business. I started to look for anything, for a green grocery shop, for a milk bar, this was the trend at that stage that every Holocaust survivor, because they didn't speak the language and because they would try for milk bars and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Janet was born, Abram was working, I mean, I was working at home and he was working. And we were looking for businesses all the time. Then one day I was told that there is a business in Acland street, which was kind of a chocolate milk bar type. And we decided to buy it. But how do you buy a business?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have enough money. But the worst part was that I have two little children. And a milk bar, it's a seven days business. So what we did, we bought it with a partner, where the woman, my friend's wife was sitting home and minding the children, mine too, especially the younger one, Janet, that was 18 months. I run the business. And it was quite successful for the first couple of years. Because I was everywhere. I was in the kitchen and outside. But then I became sick. I got so-- partial obstruction of the bowel, that is a kind of hepatitis, with-- with complications. And I went to hospital. And the two men were not able to run the business without me. Because I was in the kitchen, and the business went down, and down. And then when I came back, so when I came out of hospital, I became pregnant then with Barbara. I had Barbara, and then I went full time into the business. When Barbara was six months old, I took a housekeeper, I took a lady. I mean it wasn't a great housekeeper. But we rented a bigger apartment, and she looked after the children. And I was able so to run the business. And this is where we made big renovations. We paid out the partner, because he was-- he couldn't work. He wasn't well, and his wife wasn't willing to look after the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we bought it off him. We bought his share. And we bought the first espresso machine. And we really, Scheherazade became then what it is now already. We like people, and people liked us. And we became-- so we used to parlay around to where people coming from the theater, we were always-- it was like-- it was a cafe for an art cafe, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: People would come. We had a lot of people of Jewish people from Germany that were used to that life, the cafe life. And they would sit every afternoon in Scheherazade. Then I had a lot of Holocaust survivors. Really, that was one of the things that Scheherazade became famous. I had a group of people, perhaps there were 40 people, single people, that lost their wives or lost their girlfriends in the Holocaust, or came here before the war, and lost their-- and they were very lonely. And so when I was cooking, I had always in mind this group of people. We had special prices for them. And it-- Scheherazade became so quite successful because I always knew that this is a group of people that I really can rely on, that [NON-ENGLISH] would be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why is Scheherazade unique to other restaurants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scheherazade, that's right. It is-- It was very unique at the time where we made it, because Australian-- you know, I missed, with all the problems that we had, I still missed Europe very much when I came here. You know, I was used to Poland that you always-- you know, your life was so you would meet people in a coffee lounge, or you go to people. I was three years in Paris, which even not with good conditions, you still meet or come to-- maybe because we were living in a very small hotel room, you used to meet people and talk to people, and you used to go for the last few pennies that you had, you-- you would go to the opera. And you know, Australia was a terrible quiet place, where everybody would stay at home. And people-- you wouldn't see-- and now it's a completely-- it's marvelous. Because you walk to [NON-ENGLISH], then you see a lot of people. But 1950 there were hardly people in the street. And what we did, we brought a lot of life to Acland Street. Because really Acland Street did not have a place-- I have now the first menu that we made. And so-- that was the menu-- that was the food that my mother used to cook in Merke whatever she could. And I learned it from her, really, like all Jewish dishes, borscht and potatoes, or Russian borscht or latkes, which we in Siberia, my mother would make all different kinds of potatoes. And the latkes were part of it, you know. They were able to-- if we only had a bit of oil, she would fry the potato cakes. And this is what I brought in-- into sort of like the kitchen. I think were one of the first restaurants. There was, at that stage, there was Pelligrini, together with us. The Pelligrini brought in Italian food. And we brought in that continental Jewish Eastern Europe in-- into Acland Street. And Scheherazade was very successful because of all that, like people would come and see us. And we would sit and talk about theater, about cinema, and about the books that we read. I remember when the discussions. And this is what kept me going. Because I wouldn't be able to stay just in a milk bar. You know, I needed people. I needed conversation. And I remember when the book, came, so-- and everybody was reading And I sort of was a specialist on that [INAUDIBLE]. And we would-- for weeks we would discuss and the situation in Russia-- and it was-- and since then we sort of never looked back. We had-- I had my-- my problems. But at least it was something that financially we were successful. But it was much more than that financial success. Of course, it was very important that I could feed my children. But it was also very important that we made a kind of a social life there. We attracted a lot of artists, a lot of top artists used to come, Tucker, Albert Tucker would come regularly to Scheherazade. Look, I got tickets to the opera from Joan Sutherland. You know, when they were putting on, at the Palais, she was singing and you couldn't get the tickets. For-- for-- for a fortune you couldn't buy a ticket. My husband asked her. She was having lunch. So my husband said that I would love to hear her, and I got a ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is a lot-- really, we made a contribution. But it was a wonderful thing for us. And it is like that up to now. Did you also get involved with any other organizations or-- you know-- in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband was involved-- this was really a tradition in his house where his father and mother were always involved with Jewish causes, so was my father always involved after the war. My father was a Jewish committee, and my mother used to cook food, and I would take to hospitals, to people that came back-- to young people that came from the holocaust. Because there was a lot of TB around. And I later on met people that we helped this way that were in Israel that remember me bringing them food. So we both had a tradition of it. But the first years in Australia, were-- I think Abram started from the beginning. He never-- he never sort of gave up. Good or bad, he immediately got involved. I got very involved the moment I cooked. I mean I got involved before even Russian migrants started to come. I used to make a lot of appeals, drawing-room meetings, constantly. Yeah, I was very involved with Jewish Welfare always. But when Russian migrants started to come, I was the first Russian translator. Because Jewish Welfare was a small organization then. And Walter Lippman asked me, when the first Russian-- the first Russian family came, Walter Lippman ask me, could I translate. And of course, Russian was a language that I knew very well, so I started to go into homes of Russian migrants. And I started to translate and to help, started to find them jobs. And in 1980, I formed-- we had then a lot of Russians that were very unhappy, tried to commit suicide. And I had a case where-- and this was also my-- the time when I had a terrible tragedy in my life, where my daughter was killed in a car accident, my 17-year-old daughter. And I couldn't go near a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was asked directly from Jewish Welfare, only a few months after, 1977. And they said, could I go that a woman tried to commit suicide. And she had a big problem. Could I try to speak to her? And at the beginning, I said, no. I can't go into Alfred hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I decided I've got to go. And I started then. And then I really, in a way, I saved a woman. Because it was a big tragedy for her also. And she was very depressed. She didn't want to live. And I went, and there was a lot of Russian migrants with problems. And in 1980, I started something which is called the Tuesday Club. Because I've decided that it's enough to wait until they tried to commit suicide that we have to have some kind of prevention. Maybe we can organize a group, and this is when we organized the Tuesday Club. And it's been a very successful business up to now, so it's not a business. But we treat it like a business. You know, I've got a very dedicated group of volunteers. And we have on Tuesdays we have almost 200 people, which we thought we make them lunch. And we-- they have lectures. And it's really a mixture of Polish people that were here many years, and a lot of Russian migrants. I think it's almost one only a Jewish sort of organization, with a Russian migrant mix freely with the non-Russian. Because Russian migrants are very used to mix mostly with Russian migrants. So I really do put in a lot of work. I'm on many committees at Jewish Welfare. And this is, at this point, this is a very important part of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What challenges have you faced as a result of your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think, I don't know what challenges. But I think that a lot of strengths came. When I see now the younger generation, I often wonder, if I could so-- if I wouldn't have all those experiences that I have, if I wouldn't have to grow up fast, and if I wouldn't have to be strong as I was, I don't know how I would survive. You know, because there is still many more things that we were facing. I remember now that you ask me that question, I remember a story. That was in Merke. And I was, at that stage, I don't know. I was 15. And I always had problems with my throat, and I had an abscess. And after that, there was no doctor. The closest doctor, the nearest was some 7-8 kilometers away. So my parents said to me, well, you have to go to see a doctor. It was a terrible, hot day. Every time there is a hot day, I-- I go back and think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said to me, every time you come to some water, whatever you see, put a cold towel on your head, and this is how I did. Well, when I think now, I don't think that any parent would let a 15-year-old girl go seven kilometers with-- I don't know if I had temperature. But I definitely was pretty sick. And I came to the surgery, and they operated on me. So they cut the abscess. And I came back the same day in very hot weather. And after that, I got hepatitis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was thinking that I'm dying. And I was sitting there crying. And to me, when I think, those things make me strong. I mean they were teaching me that if you have to do something there's no way you've got to do it. So I think that this is, if you can call it whatever, a lesson or not a lesson. But this is how people survive, when they have to learn things the hard way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, do you tend to mix with other survivors, and you talk often of your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we, at this point, the first years in Australia, the first, we only mixed with Holocaust survivors. Because, first of all, we didn't speak English. Second, we had much more in common. You know, we would sit for hours. And everybody would talk about his experiences. I have quite a few people that were the same circumstances in Russia. But I heard a lot that were in concentration camps, and that-- and we would constantly-- my house was always full of Holocaust survivors, of talking about their experiences. At this point, I have a lot of people-- a lot of friends that were born here already. Because I feel quite fluent, and sort of mastered English. And I also so-- educated myself, you know, I do the psychology degree. And I have a lot of-- in these years. So I can already mix freely with other people also. But at the beginning, it was only Holocaust survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the psychology degree you just mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I did a lot of courses. And you know, I couldn't do-- I never had enough time to sit down and to make a university degree full stop. Because I was always running the business and had three children. But always, all these years I did psychology, I did philosophy. I did-- I also when my children were studying this is the interesting thing. When my children were studying, I studied with them together. You know, when my daughter did medicine, she would have here people. And she would always-- I was the examiner. And like I would-- I was quite-- thought I knew a bit. And I also was interested in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there message or lesson you'd like to pass on to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's not much-- not much of a message that one can pass on. I mean what I did, I tried-- I tried-- and my children are very educated. Because I was thinking always what I couldn't achieve, let them achieve. I think that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add to any part of this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think more or less we've covered everything. And I don't know. Maybe I've forgotten something. But I think it's almost covered.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'd like to thank you very much for telling me your story. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me who is in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my parents, my father my mother. And the photograph was taken somewhere around 1937, when we used to go for summer holidays to the mountains. We used to go for three months every summer. And they are wearing sort, which is called gura, those are the people that were living in mountains. They're wearing their costumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a lot of photographs. Actual, this one was left, my father when my father died, he had a lot of photographs. We broke them out of Poland. When we-- when I told you, remember the story was books. We also took a lot of photographs and documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken somewhere around 1932. It's myself and my brother, taken in Sosnowiec. This is a document taken in 1947. It's sort-- a document from-- from-- the Katowice, from the committee in Katowice. This is a document taken at-- when I entered Lodge University. So this is a document from Lodge University. This is 1948. And this is the fifth anniversary of the Warsaw ghetto uprising. Those are the students from Lodge that came to demonstrate. And as you see, I wear the white hat. This is medical and medicine. And the other student are polytechnic, with the red heads. In the middle is Abram. This is actually our wedding photograph, if you can say. This is taking in Paris 1949. It's Abram and myself. This is our first photograph in Australia. It's my sister, Abram in the middle, holding John, and myself on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken when we came, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is John in a splint. The photograph was taken in Mount Eliza somewhere around 1953. John was 2 and 1/2 years almost in hospital in Mount Eliza, in Children's Hospital. This is a photograph taken when President Herzog visited Australia. I am sort of the first to the right. And it was a reception for him, which we attended. And it was a beautiful reception. This is an award that I got in '94 from the city of Caulfield for working so-- for doing volunteer work. This is an award that I got from Jewish Welfare for running the Tuesday Club, and being involved in Jewish Welfare for that many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my family. It's Abram, my husband; Ariel, and Annie, David, my daughter, Janet. And in the back Sarah, in the back, Sarah, Ashley, my daughter-in-law Eva, and my son John.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why you tell my daddy, John?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just got a question for the children. Maybe I'll ask Janet first. Were you aware of what your parents experienced when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that manifest itself, or how-- how did it come across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think my father was very keen on imparting his experiences, and we certainly always commemorated ghetto uprisings, and we were very involved in kind of learning about Jewish history, but particularly the history of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, anything you'd like to add to that question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I was probably even more so, because I'm the oldest of three. So, hence, I was born much closer to that experience. I was born five years after the end of the Holocaust. And-- I mean there is one very interesting story, when in fact, I was on crutches. Because I-- probably had [INAUDIBLE] disease. And there was one very interesting story in print, where I actually hit a little boy at school with crutches. And the only reason why, I mean we hadn't any personal dispute, because he was German. So obviously there was a lot that had been said to me at that time. Which was probably fairly rare. Because a lot of people coming through that generation had not told their stories. And one of my hypothesis for that, is that in fact, in particular my father had had a very positive experience, if you can call it that, during the Holocaust, compared to what [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know that you'd call it positive. I think what was important was that he was active, rather than passive. And I think that probably contributed far more to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think rather than dad's experience being positive, I think his experience was someone who was actually active and able to exert some autonomy, unlike most other people, well, certainly most other people who, you know, were incarcerated and very little choice about whatever actions they could take. And I think that helped him probably feel stronger about his experiences, and therefore more willing to discuss them, and more willing to talk about them, as opposed to people who were concentration camp survivors who tend to remain silent, I think because of the shame associated with their experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how have your parents' experiences influence the way you bring up your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jellybeans.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question to answer, in that I-- I don't know how I would have done it, had one not have gone though those experiences, I mean their experiences. And-- and I think-- I mean they were very important ones. I mean my father can answer the question as to whether it was positive. I agree it wasn't a pleasant experience. But it certainly molded his character in as far as he was-- he was a war hero. It was something that he did very-- very early on in his life. And I think if you questioned him, you'd find he almost look back in a nostalgic way. That period, not for the events that occurred, but to some of the times and the camaraderie and things like that. Obviously, I mean, there is a very strong identification with Judaism that a lot people would have come through. But it's not really clear. Because some people who went through almost the same circumstances wanted to run away, because their attitude was why did this happen to me. What did I do to deserve that. So in that way, I think we've got a very positive sort of education, and would to continue with that. In particular, because the-- the culture that he came from was significantly destroyed. But [INAUDIBLE] didn't exist. And so it has been very, very difficult for people from that generation who came from a socialist democratic movement who were not Zionists. They really led a fairly empty and shallow life. And a lot-- and a lot them having, in fact, not been very committed to Judaism. Because their sense of Judaism no longer exists. But that's certainly been a difference as far as I'm concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything anyone else would like to add? Mr. Zeleznikow, would you like to say anything? You gave your testimony last week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got my testimony last week. To this question is-- I think that both of my children understand very well. It's not a question of a positive or a negative, and not that you have been able to decide by yourself. Because nobody in the time of the [NON-ENGLISH], was able to decide about themselves. Everything was [INAUDIBLE] by the Nazis, and [INAUDIBLE]. The question that I think they understand very well is this was my youth. And everybody better use or worse use, is always remember Shoah. And being in a fighting organization, made you feel stronger. And looking back, which I wouldn't say some nostalgia nor with some pride that you have been able to live, to fight, and to continue your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all, very much. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zeleznikow actually gave his testimony last week. But we're just putting on the photographs on his wife's tape. Mr. Zeleznikow, could you please tell me who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a sketch, made by a well known Jewish French painter, Isaac Dobrinsky, of my father in year 1928 in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a second sketch of my father made by the same painter in the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Ethel Stok Zeleznikow, what was the director for a Jewish children's home for orphans in Vilna in the '30s, together with a historian [NON-ENGLISH], on the right, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Can you? I think a little bit my sister's head is-- this is the only photograph of my family. That's my father, Yankel; my mother, Ethel; my sister, Batya; and myself, when I am only two years old. And the photo is made in 1926 in Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Batya Zeleznikow Taubman. At least in the beginning of the '30s. My sister was 13 years older than me when she was killed by the liquidation of the Vilna ghetto in the 23rd of September, 1943.
